title: Somatic Therapy for Workaholism: When Busyness Is a Survival Strategy
excerpt: Explore how somatic therapy reveals workaholism as a trauma survival strategy and offers body-based tools to reclaim calm and presence.
body:
Understanding Workaholism Beyond Productivity

Workaholism is often framed as a personality flaw or a harmless habit, but what if it’s actually a survival strategy rooted in trauma and nervous system dysregulation? For many, relentless busyness is less about ambition and more about managing overwhelming internal experiences. Somatic therapy offers a way to understand and heal this pattern by tuning into the body’s signals instead of just the mind’s narratives.

What Is Somatic Therapy?

Somatic therapy focuses on the body’s role in processing trauma and regulating the nervous system. Unlike traditional talk therapy, which centers on thoughts and feelings, somatic approaches engage bodily sensations, movements, and breath to access and release stored stress and trauma. Pioneers like Peter Levine and Pat Ogden have demonstrated that trauma isn’t just in the mind—it’s held in the body’s defenses and survival responses.

Workaholism as a Trauma Survival Strategy

Many people who identify as workaholics have underlying experiences of early life stress, attachment wounds, or chronic overwhelm. The constant drive to work can be a way to avoid uncomfortable feelings like anxiety, shame, or emptiness. By keeping busy, the nervous system remains in a state of hyperarousal or dissociation, preventing full awareness of painful internal states.

According to Bessel van der Kolk, trauma survivors often use strategies that keep the nervous system activated to avoid sensations related to helplessness or fear. Busyness can serve as a distraction and a way to feel in control when other parts of life feel unsafe or unpredictable.

The Nervous System and Workaholism

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory provides a useful framework for understanding how the nervous system influences workaholic behaviors. The nervous system has three main states: safe/social engagement, fight/flight, and shutdown/freezing. Workaholism often reflects a nervous system stuck in fight/flight or high sympathetic arousal—constantly mobilized to do, achieve, and avoid vulnerability.

When the body is in this state, it’s difficult to rest or connect deeply with oneself and others. The parasympathetic system, responsible for rest and digestion, is suppressed. Deb Dana’s work highlights how restoring a sense of safety in the body is key to shifting out of these survival modes.

Somatic Tools to Address Workaholism

Somatic therapy offers practical, body-based tools to help break the cycle of busyness and reclaim regulation:

  • Body Awareness: Tracking bodily sensations during moments of urge to overwork helps identify underlying emotions like anxiety or shame.
  • Breath Regulation: Slow, diaphragmatic breathing activates the parasympathetic nervous system, helping downregulate hyperarousal.
  • Movement and Grounding: Gentle movements or grounding exercises can release tension and enhance a sense of safety.
  • Pendulation: Developed by Peter Levine, this involves moving between states of discomfort and safety to build tolerance for difficult sensations.

Why Traditional Therapy May Not Be Enough

Many people with workaholism have tried talk therapy without lasting change. This is because cognitive insight alone rarely rewires the nervous system’s conditioned survival patterns. Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy and Francine Shapiro’s EMDR show how integrating body-based techniques with psychotherapy can access deep-held trauma and support lasting transformation.

Gabor Maté emphasizes that healing addictive patterns like workaholism requires compassion for the trauma embedded in the behavior—not judgment or willpower alone.

Steps Toward Self-Directed Somatic Healing for Workaholism

If you’ve recognized your own workaholism as a survival strategy, here are steps to begin somatic healing on your own:

  • Create Safe Spaces: Identify moments in your day where you can pause and check in with your body without distraction.
  • Practice Gentle Breathwork: Use 4-6 breaths per minute to engage the parasympathetic system.
  • Notice Sensations: When you feel the urge to overwork, pause and ask, "Where do I feel this in my body?"
  • Allow Movement: Stretch, shake, or walk to release accumulated tension.
  • Journal Bodily Experiences: Write about sensations and emotions that arise, helping to integrate body and mind.

When to Seek Professional Somatic Therapy

While self-directed practices can help, deep-seated trauma and nervous system dysregulation often require guidance from an experienced somatic therapist. Professionals trained in approaches like Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, Somatic Experiencing, or EMDR can help safely navigate intense sensations and emotional release.

Dan Siegel highlights the importance of "mindsight," the ability to observe one’s internal experience with curiosity and care—a skill often cultivated in therapeutic settings.

“Workaholism isn’t just about working too much. It’s about how the body tries to survive unbearable feelings by staying constantly ‘on.’ Somatic therapy helps us come home to ourselves.” — The Oracle Lover

Conclusion: Reclaiming Balance Through the Body

Workaholism as a survival strategy is a common but misunderstood phenomenon. It’s not a personal failure but a sign that the nervous system is trying to protect you from pain. Somatic therapy offers a path to reconnect with your body, regulate your nervous system, and find balance beyond busyness. By learning to feel safe within, you can move from survival to thriving.

Remember, as Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems model suggests, different parts of ourselves may hold conflicting roles—some pushing us to work, others craving rest. Somatic work helps bring harmony between these parts through embodied awareness.

Healing workaholism is possible. It begins with listening to what your body is trying to tell you and responding with compassion and curiosity.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy differ from traditional talk therapy for workaholism?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on the body’s sensations and nervous system regulation, helping to release trauma held physically. Traditional therapy often emphasizes cognitive insight, which may not address the body’s role in survival behaviors like workaholism.

faqs.q: Can I practice somatic healing techniques on my own?
faqs.a: Yes, many somatic tools like breathwork, body awareness, and grounding can be practiced self-directed. However, deep trauma often benefits from guidance by a trained somatic therapist.

faqs.q: Why do some people use busyness to cope with trauma?
faqs.a: Busyness keeps the nervous system in a state of activation, which can distract from painful emotions or memories. It serves as a survival strategy to avoid vulnerability and overwhelming feelings.

faqs.q: What are signs that somatic therapy might be right for me?
faqs.a: If you feel stuck despite therapy, notice physical tension or overwhelm linked to work habits, or struggle to feel calm and present, somatic therapy could support deeper healing.

affiliateProducts.description: Peter Levine’s foundational book explains how trauma is held in the body and introduces somatic experiencing techniques.

affiliateProducts.title: Polyvagal Exercises for Safety and Connection
affiliateProducts.description: Deb Dana offers practical exercises grounded in Polyvagal Theory to help regulate the nervous system and cultivate safety.
